author: King James I
work: The True Law of Free Monarchies
notes: Kings are God's lieutenants on earth; subjects owe obedience; the king above positive law but under divine/natural law; the coronation oath treated; coins the key arguments used by later royalists

author: King James I
notes: Written for his son Prince Henry; the king's duties before God; the coronation oath in detail — the king's oath is to God first, then to his people

author: Robert Filmer
work: Patriarcha (or The Natural Power of Kings)
notes: Monarchy grounded in Adam's paternal authority; OT genealogy of kingship; most systematic royalist text; Locke wrote both Treatises specifically to refute this

author: Robert Filmer
work: The Necessity of the Absolute Power of All Kings
notes: Compact collection of arguments from Scripture and history

author: Jean Bodin
work: Six Books of the Commonwealth (Les Six Livres de la République)
notes: Foundational modern theory of sovereignty; sovereignty as indivisible; monarchy as its natural and best form

author: Jacques-Bénigne Bossuet
work: Politics Drawn from the Very Words of Holy Scripture
notes: The most thorough biblical defense of monarchy ever written; every proposition grounded in OT text; royal authority is sacred paternal absolute (but not arbitrary)

author: Bossuet
notes: God works through great monarchs; history as the unfolding of divine providence through kings

author: Richard Hooker
work: Of the Laws of Ecclesiastical Polity
notes: Consent expressed through and within divinely ordered hierarchy; ordered allegiance not democratic consent

author: Hugo Grotius
notes: Oaths binding in natural law; the oath to a sovereign as a natural law obligation; breaking it is perjury before God not just breach of contract

author: Samuel Rutherford
notes: Written against absolute monarchy; the king under law; important counterpoint to Filmer/Bossuet; but affirms monarchy as a form

author: William Barclay
notes: Defense of royal power against resistance theorists; coined the term 'monarchomach'
